sentence: HPI: The patient presents with chronic back pain that started approximately two years ago after moving furniture, described as a sharp stabbing pain in the back that radiates down to the right leg and even to the big toe, with numbness and tingling in the right leg, difficulty getting comfortable, especially when sitting, and a feeling of being unable to be as active as he would like.
He has tried physical therapy, strong pain medications, and has been considered for surgery but would like to avoid it.
He also reports weight gain due to decreased activity level.
Past medical history: The patient denies any family members with spine conditions and has a history of smoking, quitting approximately 20 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient reports numbness and tingling in the right leg, but denies any loss of sensation in the genital or rectal area, weakness, loss of bladder or bowel control.

Physical exam: The patient has midline tenderness at L4-L5 disk space with right-sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness, pain with lumbar flexion, positive straight leg test, decreased sensation to light touch at the right lateral thigh at L4 dermatome, and symmetrical patellar and achilles reflexes.
Test Results: MRI results show disk herniation with nerve impingement.
Assessment: The patient has symptoms consistent with an L4-L5 herniated disk with radiculopathy.
Problem list: Herniated disk; Radiculopathy; Chronic back pain; Weight gain.
Plan: I prescribed a spinal injection with a corticosteroid to help reduce inflammation and alleviate pain.
The injection will be administered under fluoroscopy to accurately target the area of pain.
I will monitor the patient's response to the injection and schedule follow-up appointments as needed, with a maximum of four injections per year.
Follow-up: The patient will return for a follow-up appointment after the first injection to assess response and schedule subsequent injections as needed.
